verses.commentary: Melchizedek was king of Salem and priest of God Most High. He met Abraham returning from defeating the kings and blessed him. Genesis 14 provides the narrative. Melchizedek appears suddenly, without genealogy or context, making him a fitting type of Christ's eternal priesthood.

Abraham gave him a tenth of everything. His name means king of righteousness; king of Salem means king of peace. The names are theologically loaded: righteousness and peace characterize Messiah's reign. These are not accidents but prophetic signposts.

Without father or mother, without genealogy, without beginning of days or end of life, resembling the Son of God, he remains a priest forever. Not that Melchizedek was literally eternal, but Scripture's silence about his origins makes him a type of Christ's eternal priesthood. The textual silence is purposeful.

Just think how great he was: even Abraham, the patriarch, gave him a tenth of the plunder. Abraham's act of tithing to Melchizedek establishes Melchizedek's superiority. The greater blesses the lesser; the lesser tithes to the greater.

Levitical priests collect tithes from their fellow Israelites according to the Law. Even though Levites descended from Abraham, they collect from their brothers. The Levitical system includes tithing as part of its structure.

But Melchizedek, who did not trace descent from Levi, collected a tenth from Abraham and blessed him who had the promises. Melchizedek operates outside the Levitical system yet is superior to it. He blessed Abraham, the promise-bearer.
Without doubt the lesser is blessed by the greater. This is an axiomatic principle. Blessing flows from superior to inferior. Melchizedek's blessing of Abraham proves his superiority.

In one case, the tenth is collected by people who die; in the other case, by him who is declared to be living. Levitical priests died and were replaced. Melchizedek's priesthood, by textual silence, is perpetual. This points to Christ's eternal priesthood.

One might even say that Levi, who collects the tenth, paid the tenth through Abraham. Corporate solidarity: Levi was in Abraham's loins when Abraham tithed to Melchizedek. Thus, even Levi acknowledged Melchizedek's superiority.

Because when Melchizedek met Abraham, Levi was still in the body of his ancestor. The argument from representation: Abraham's actions implicate his descendants. This establishes Melchizedek's (and thus Christ's) superiority over the Levitical order.

If perfection could have been attained through the Levitical priesthood (on which the Law was based), why was there still need for another priest to come in the order of Melchizedek, not Aaron? The need for a new priesthood proves the inadequacy of the old. If Levitical priesthood worked, no replacement would be needed.
For when the priesthood is changed, the law must be changed also. Priesthood and Law are inseparable. A new priesthood requires a new covenant. Christ's priesthood inaugurates the New Covenant.

He of whom these things are said belonged to a different tribe, and no one from that tribe has ever served at the altar. Jesus was from Judah, not Levi. Under the old system, He was disqualified from priesthood. But the new order transcends tribal restrictions.

For it is clear that our Lord descended from Judah, and in regard to that tribe Moses said nothing about priests. Judah was the kingly tribe, not the priestly tribe. Jesus combines kingship and priesthood, fulfilling Melchizedek's dual role.
What we have said is even more clear if another priest like Melchizedek appears. The argument reaches its climax. Jesus is the Melchizedek-like priest who fulfills Psalm 110:4.

One who has become a priest not on the basis of a regulation as to his ancestry but on the basis of the power of an indestructible life. Christ's priesthood is not genealogical but ontological—based on who He is (the eternal Son with indestructible life), not ancestry.
For it is declared: You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek. Psalm 110:4 seals the argument. Forever means Christ's priesthood never ends, never needs replacement.
The former regulation is set aside because it was weak and useless. The Law could not perfect anyone. It was not bad but inadequate. It could not accomplish what it pointed toward.

For the law made nothing perfect, and a better hope is introduced, by which we draw near to God. The Law's function was preparatory. Christ introduces better hope—actual access to God, not mere symbolic approach.
And it was not without an oath! Others became priests without any oath. Levitical priests were appointed by birth, not oath. No divine oath confirmed their priesthood.

But He became a priest with an oath when God said to him: The Lord has sworn and will not change His mind: You are a priest forever. Psalm 110:4 includes divine oath. God's unchangeable oath guarantees Christ's eternal priesthood.
Because of this oath, Jesus has become the guarantor of a better covenant. Oath-backed priesthood secures oath-backed covenant. Jesus personally guarantees the New Covenant's promises.

Now there have been many of those priests, since death prevented them from continuing in office. Levitical priesthood required constant succession. Death interrupted service. Mortality plagued the old system.

But because Jesus lives forever, He has a permanent priesthood. Indestructible life means uninterrupted service. He never dies, never retires, never needs replacement. His priesthood is unchangeable (aparabaton).

Therefore He is able to save completely those who come to God through Him, because He always lives to intercede for them. Complete salvation (to the uttermost) is guaranteed by eternal intercession. He saves fully because He lives perpetually. Our salvation depends not on our faithfulness but His.

Such a high priest truly meets our need—one who is holy, blameless, pure, set apart from sinners, exalted above the heavens. Moral perfection qualifies Him to represent us. He is sinless yet sympathetic, exalted yet accessible.

Unlike other high priests, He does not need to offer sacrifices day after day, first for His own sins, then for the people. He sacrificed for their sins once for all when He offered Himself. No need for daily sacrifices or personal atonement. His self-offering was sufficient and final.

For the law appoints as high priests men in all their weakness; but the oath, which came after the law, appointed the Son, who has been made perfect forever. The contrast concludes: Law appoints weak men; oath appoints the perfect Son. Christ's priesthood is final and sufficient.
